name: Honduras
tagline: From the Maya kingdom of Copán and the Lenca resistance to a Central American republic shaped by federation, fruit companies, and democratic struggle.
summary: Honduras occupies a heartland of ancient Mesoamerica, where the Maya city of Copán raised the longest hieroglyphic inscription in the Americas before its abandonment around 1200. After the Spanish arrived in 1502 and overcame the resistance led by the Lenca chief Lempira, the territory became a silver-mining province of the Captaincy General of Guatemala. Honduras declared independence from Spain on 15 September 1821, passed briefly through Mexican annexation and the Federal Republic of Central America championed by Francisco Morazán, then emerged as a sovereign state in 1838. Its modern history has been marked by the banana economy and U.S. fruit companies, long stretches of military rule, the devastation of Hurricane Mitch in 1998, the political crisis of 2009, and a twenty-first century shaped by migration and a contested democratic life.

founding.label: Independence from Spain
founding.detail: On 15 September 1821 the provinces of the Captaincy General of Guatemala, Honduras among them, declared their independence from Spain. Honduras was briefly drawn into the short-lived Mexican Empire, then in 1823 joined the United Provinces (later Federal Republic) of Central America. The federation fractured amid conflict between Liberals and Conservatives, and Honduras separated to become a fully sovereign state in 1838. The United States recognised Honduras as a distinct independent nation on 19 April 1853.

quickFacts.label: Ancient capital
quickFacts.value: Maya city of Copán, peak population c. 20,000 in the 9th century

quickFacts.label: Indigenous resistance
quickFacts.value: Lenca chief Lempira, who unified peoples against Spain in 1537

quickFacts.label: Colonial framework
quickFacts.value: Captaincy General of Guatemala; silver mining at Tegucigalpa

eras.title: Copán and the First Peoples
eras.standfirst: In western Honduras the Maya raised the kingdom of Copán, with its dynasty of sixteen kings and the longest hieroglyphic stairway of the ancient Americas, while the Lenca and other peoples held the rest of the land.
body: Copán began as a small agricultural settlement about 1000 BCE and grew into one of the great Maya cities of the Classic Period, flourishing roughly between 250 and 900 CE on the west bank of the Copán River near the modern Guatemalan border. A dynasty of at least sixteen kings ruled the city from about 426 to 822, and at its early ninth-century peak Copán may have held as many as twenty thousand inhabitants. Its central district of stone temples, pyramids, stairways, and plazas is celebrated above all for its carved stelae and friezes, among the finest sculpture of the Maya world.

The most famous monument is the Hieroglyphic Stairway, whose risers bear some twelve hundred carved glyphs recording the dynastic history of Copán's rulers, their accessions, deaths, and defining deeds. It is the longest known Maya hieroglyphic text. Begun under the fourteenth ruler and completed about 755 by the fifteenth, the stairway and the surrounding monuments were inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1980. Copán's population dwindled in the ninth century, and the site was completely abandoned by about 1200.

Beyond the Maya zone, Honduras was home to a mosaic of peoples. The Lenca occupied the central and southern highlands; the Pech (Paya), Tolupan, and Miskito lived in the east and along the Caribbean. In the dense rainforests of the Mosquitia, watered by the Río Plátano, these communities developed ways of life adapted to one of the last great expanses of tropical forest in Central America, a landscape later recognised by UNESCO as the Río Plátano Biosphere Reserve.

events.title: Copán settled
events.summary: A small farming village on the Copán River grows over centuries into a major Maya centre in what is now western Honduras.

events.title: Dynasty of sixteen kings
events.summary: A line of at least sixteen rulers governs Copán, raising temples, stelae, and the Hieroglyphic Stairway during the city's Classic-period height.

events.title: Hieroglyphic Stairway completed
events.summary: The fifteenth ruler finishes the great stairway, whose roughly 1,200 carved glyphs form the longest known Maya inscription.

events.title: Copán abandoned
events.summary: After centuries of decline the Maya completely abandon Copán, leaving its monuments to the forest.

pullquote.text: The Hieroglyphic Stairway is beautifully carved with some 1,260 hieroglyphic symbols on the risers of its remaining steps.

eras.title: Conquest, Lempira, and Colonial Silver
eras.standfirst: Spanish forces overcame the resistance of the Lenca chief Lempira and bound Honduras into the Captaincy General of Guatemala, where the silver strikes around Tegucigalpa shaped a poor and thinly settled colony.
body: Christopher Columbus reached the Honduran coast in 1502 on his fourth voyage, but sustained conquest did not begin until the 1520s, when expeditions sent by Hernán Cortés and rival captains pushed inland. Resistance was fierce. In 1537 the Lenca chief Lempira drew together more than two hundred indigenous communities to oppose the Spanish advance from a stronghold in the western highlands. His revolt was eventually broken, and a major war between Spaniards and the native population in 1537 ended in the decimation of the indigenous peoples through warfare, disease, and enslavement. Lempira endures as a national hero, his name carried by the Honduran currency.

Honduras was incorporated into the Captaincy General of Guatemala, the colonial administration that linked the provinces of Central America to the Spanish crown. The gold-mining town of Gracias briefly served as the seat of the Audiencia de los Confines in the 1540s. The colony's fortunes turned on minerals: a silver strike in the highlands in the 1570s drew prospectors and gave rise to Tegucigalpa, which would rival the older provincial capital of Comayagua, especially through the eighteenth century.

Yet Honduras remained one of the poorer and more sparsely populated corners of the Spanish empire. Mining was intermittent and labour scarce, the indigenous population had collapsed, and the Caribbean coast lay largely beyond Spanish control, exposed to English logwood cutters, pirates, and the Miskito of the Mosquito Coast. Three centuries of colonial rule left a small Spanish-speaking, Catholic society concentrated in the interior highlands, its economy tied to silver and cattle rather than the plantation wealth of richer colonies.

events.title: Columbus reaches Honduras
events.summary: On his fourth voyage Christopher Columbus makes landfall on the Caribbean coast of Honduras.

events.title: Lempira's resistance
events.summary: The Lenca chief Lempira unites more than two hundred communities against the Spanish before his revolt is crushed.

events.title: Silver strike and rise of Tegucigalpa
events.summary: A highland silver discovery draws prospectors and gives rise to Tegucigalpa, long a rival to Comayagua.

figures.name: Lempira
figures.blurb: Warrior chief of the Lenca who in 1537 united more than two hundred indigenous communities in organised resistance to the Spanish conquest of the western highlands. Defeated and killed, he became Honduras's foremost national hero, and the country's currency bears his name.

pullquote.text: A major war between the Spaniards and the Indians broke out in 1537; the conflict ended in the decimation of the Indian population.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on the conquest of Honduras

eras.title: Independence, Federation, and Morazán
eras.standfirst: Independence from Spain in 1821 led through a brief Mexican annexation into the Federal Republic of Central America, whose foremost figure, the Honduran Francisco Morazán, fought to hold the union together before it dissolved.
body: On 15 September 1821 the provinces of the Captaincy General of Guatemala declared their independence from Spain, ending three centuries of colonial rule. Almost at once Honduras and its neighbours were swept into the short-lived Mexican Empire of Agustín de Iturbide. When that empire collapsed in 1823, the Central American provinces declared independence again and formed the United Provinces of Central America, later styled the Federal Republic of Central America, binding together the lands that are now Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua, and Costa Rica under a federal constitution adopted in 1824.

The federation's brief life was dominated by the Honduran soldier and statesman Francisco Morazán, born in Tegucigalpa in 1792. Leading the Liberal cause, he defeated the Conservatives and was elected president of the United Provinces, serving through most of the 1830s. Morazán pressed reforms to limit the privileges of the Roman Catholic Church and to build a modern liberal state, but his programme provoked fierce Conservative opposition and a succession of revolts that consumed his administration.

Friction between Liberals and Conservatives, regional rivalries, and the absence of a shared national feeling proved fatal to the union. The federation began to disintegrate in the late 1830s, and Honduras formally separated to become a sovereign state in 1838. Morazán's later attempt to restore the union from a base in Costa Rica failed, and he was overthrown and executed in San José on 15 September 1842, the very anniversary of independence. He remains a celebrated hero across Central America and the supreme symbol of the federal ideal.

events.title: Independence from Spain
events.summary: The provinces of the Captaincy General of Guatemala, Honduras among them, declare their independence from Spain.

events.title: Federal Republic of Central America
events.summary: After a brief Mexican annexation, Honduras joins the United Provinces of Central America under a federal constitution adopted in 1824.

events.title: Morazán leads the federation
events.summary: The Honduran Liberal Francisco Morazán serves as president of the United Provinces, fighting to preserve the union against Conservative revolts.

events.title: Honduras becomes sovereign
events.summary: Amid the federation's collapse, Honduras separates to become a fully independent state.

figures.name: Francisco Morazán
figures.role: President of the Federal Republic of Central America
figures.blurb: Honduran-born soldier and statesman, the outstanding political and military figure of Central America in his era. As Liberal president of the United Provinces he sought to build a unified, secular republic; defeated by Conservatives, he was executed in 1842 and is honoured as the champion of Central American union.

pullquote.text: Morazán was the outstanding military and political hero of Central America from 1827 until his death.

eras.title: The Banana Economy and the Original Banana Republic
eras.standfirst: From the late nineteenth century, U.S. fruit companies turned Honduras's north coast into vast banana plantations, binding the nation's economy and politics so tightly to foreign capital that the term banana republic was coined for it.
body: In the closing decades of the nineteenth century, North American entrepreneurs began clearing the lowlands of the Caribbean coast for banana cultivation. In return for large land grants and tax concessions from Honduran governments, U.S. firms built railways, ports, and company towns, and made bananas the country's overwhelming export. By the early twentieth century the trade was dominated by three great enterprises: the United Fruit Company, the Standard Fruit Company, and the Cuyamel Fruit Company.

These companies wielded enormous power over a weak and indebted state. The Cuyamel Fruit Company, founded by Samuel Zemurray, bought plantation land from 1910 and became deeply enmeshed in Honduran politics; in 1911 Zemurray backed an armed coup to install a friendly government. By 1913 the United States accounted for the great bulk of Honduran foreign trade. The dependence was so complete that the writer O. Henry, who had lived on the Honduran coast, coined the phrase banana republic to describe such a state, and Honduras became its archetype.

The plantation economy reshaped Honduran society, drawing migrant labour to the coast, creating a wage-earning workforce, and tying national fortunes to the fluctuations of a single crop and the decisions of foreign boardrooms. In 1929, as the Great Depression began, Zemurray sold Cuyamel to United Fruit, concentrating the industry in still fewer hands. The banana enclave, prosperous yet politically subordinate, left a legacy of foreign economic dominance and labour grievance that would shape Honduran politics for generations.

events.title: Rise of the fruit companies
events.summary: U.S. firms, foremost United Fruit and Standard Fruit, develop the north coast, making bananas Honduras's dominant export.

events.title: Cuyamel and the Zemurray coup
events.summary: Samuel Zemurray's Cuyamel Fruit Company buys plantation land and, in 1911, backs an armed coup to secure a favourable government.

events.title: United Fruit absorbs Cuyamel
events.summary: Zemurray sells Cuyamel to the United Fruit Company, concentrating the Honduran banana industry as the Depression begins.

pullquote.text: The term banana republic was coined for Honduras, whose economy and politics were dominated by foreign fruit companies.
pullquote.attribution: After O. Henry's coinage, on Honduras

eras.title: Military Rule and the Cold-War Frontier
eras.standfirst: Through much of the twentieth century Honduras was governed by the military or by leaders it sustained; in the 1980s it became a U.S. staging ground in the Central American conflicts, before Hurricane Mitch closed the century in catastrophe.
body: The long dictatorship of Tiburcio Carías Andino from 1933 to 1949 inaugurated decades in which the armed forces stood at the centre of national life. A landmark banana workers' strike in 1954 galvanised the labour movement and pressed for reform, but power alternated between elected civilians and military officers, with the army repeatedly seizing or shaping government. A brief 1969 border war with neighbouring El Salvador, sparked amid migration and a football tournament, further entrenched the military's prestige and authority.

In the 1980s Honduras became a strategic frontier in the United States' Cold-War struggle in Central America. As civil wars raged in neighbouring Nicaragua, El Salvador, and Guatemala, the U.S. expanded its presence at the Soto Cano (Palmerola) air base from 1983, using Honduran territory to support the Nicaraguan Contra rebels fighting the Sandinista government. The decade brought a militarised politics, U.S. aid and troops, and grave human-rights abuses, including disappearances attributed to the army's intelligence unit, even as civilian government was formally restored in 1982.

The era ended in disaster. In late October 1998 Hurricane Mitch, among the deadliest Atlantic hurricanes on record, stalled over Central America and drenched Honduras in torrential rain. Floods and mudslides destroyed villages, bridges, roads, and much of the country's agriculture; some seven thousand Hondurans died, and a large share of the population was left homeless. The storm set back development by years and made reconstruction and foreign debt relief the central tasks of the nation entering the new century.

events.title: Carías dictatorship
events.summary: Tiburcio Carías Andino governs as authoritarian president, opening an era of military influence over Honduran politics.

events.title: War with El Salvador
events.summary: A brief border war with El Salvador, rooted in migration disputes, erupts amid a football tournament and hardens the military's role.

events.title: Contra war staging ground
events.summary: Honduras hosts an expanded U.S. military presence at Soto Cano and serves as a base for the Nicaraguan Contras against the Sandinistas.

events.title: Hurricane Mitch
events.summary: One of the deadliest Atlantic hurricanes devastates Honduras, killing some 7,000 people and ruining infrastructure and crops.

pullquote.text: Hurricane Mitch was among the deadliest Atlantic hurricanes on record, leaving millions homeless across Central America.

eras.title: Crisis, Migration, and a New Century
eras.standfirst: The new century brought the political crisis of 2009, surging emigration and gang violence, the first woman president in 2022, and a contested election that handed power to a new president in 2026.
body: On 28 June 2009 Honduras was plunged into its gravest political crisis in decades. President Manuel Zelaya, who had moved leftward and joined the Venezuelan-led ALBA alliance, was promoting a non-binding poll on rewriting the constitution. The Supreme Court had declared the exercise illegal; on the morning of the planned vote the military detained Zelaya and flew him out of the country, and Congress installed its leader, Roberto Micheletti, as acting president. A truth commission later established by the Organization of American States concluded that Zelaya had broken the law in defying the courts but that his removal was itself an illegal coup, a judgment that captured the dispute's contested character.

The years that followed were difficult. Honduras endured some of the highest homicide rates in the world, driven by transnational gangs and the drug trade, and hundreds of thousands of Hondurans joined migrant caravans bound for the United States. In 2022 former president Juan Orlando Hernández was extradited to the United States, where he was later convicted on drug-trafficking charges, a striking symbol of the entanglement of politics and organised crime.

In November 2021 Xiomara Castro, wife of the ousted Zelaya and candidate of the Libre party, won the presidency, taking office on 27 January 2022 as the first woman to lead Honduras. Her government severed long-standing ties with Taiwan to recognise the People's Republic of China in 2023. A bitterly contested general election in November 2025 was narrowly won by Nasry Asfura of the National Party, a former mayor of Tegucigalpa, who was sworn in on 27 January 2026, succeeding Castro after she became the first left-wing president to complete a full term in the country.

events.title: Removal of President Zelaya
events.summary: The military removes President Manuel Zelaya and Congress installs Roberto Micheletti; a later OAS commission deems the removal an illegal coup while finding Zelaya had defied the courts.

events.title: Xiomara Castro inaugurated
events.summary: Xiomara Castro takes office as Honduras's first woman president, ending years of National Party rule.

events.title: Extradition of Juan Orlando Hernández
events.summary: Former president Juan Orlando Hernández is extradited to the United States to face drug-trafficking charges, later resulting in conviction.

events.title: Asfura succeeds Castro
events.summary: After a narrow, contested 2025 election, Nasry Asfura of the National Party is inaugurated president, succeeding Xiomara Castro.

figures.name: Manuel Zelaya
figures.blurb: Liberal-turned-leftist president whose push for constitutional change ended in his removal by the military and Congress on 28 June 2009, an episode later judged an illegal coup by an OAS truth commission.

figures.name: Xiomara Castro
figures.blurb: Candidate of the Libre party and wife of Manuel Zelaya, who in January 2022 became the first woman president of Honduras and the first left-wing leader to complete a full term in office.

pullquote.text: The Honduras truth commission determined that Zelaya's removal was indeed an illegal coup and not a constitutional succession.

figures.name: Lempira
figures.blurb: Lenca chief who unified more than two hundred indigenous communities against the Spanish conquest in 1537; Honduras's foremost national hero, commemorated in the name of its currency.

figures.name: Francisco Morazán
figures.role: President of the Federal Republic of Central America
figures.blurb: Honduran-born statesman and the leading figure of Central American union, who as Liberal president sought to hold the federation together before its collapse and his execution in 1842.

figures.name: Xiomara Castro
figures.blurb: Libre party leader inaugurated in January 2022 as the first woman to hold the Honduran presidency, more than a decade after the removal of her husband, President Manuel Zelaya.
